skill.name: codebase-cleanup-tech-debt
skill.description: A comprehensive skill for identifying, quantifying, and prioritizing technical debt in software projects. Provides detailed guidance on conducting debt audits, assessing impact with ROI calculations, creating prioritized remediation plans, and implementing prevention strategies.
skill.summary: Identify and prioritize technical debt with actionable remediation plans and ROI-focused strategies
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 30 static findings evaluated as false positives. The skill is purely instructional content for technical debt analysis. Dollar amounts in examples ($150/hour) were misidentified as shell commands. Generic technical terms were misidentified as cryptographic algorithms. Prevention planning was misidentified as system reconnaissance. No actual security risks present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dollar Amount Examples Misidentified
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner detected Ruby/shell backtick execution at 25 locations. Upon review, these are dollar amounts in example text (e.g., $150/hour, $36,000) within markdown code blocks - not actual shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All detected patterns are dollar amounts in example text blocks, not actual shell commands. No command execution occurs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generic Terms Misidentified as Cryptographic Algorithms
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner detected weak cryptographic algorithms at 4 locations. Upon review, these are generic technical terms in the debt analysis context (design flaws, technology debt, documentation debt, DDD patterns) - no cryptographic code exists.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Lines contain generic terms like 'design', 'architecture', 'legacy' - no cryptographic algorithms present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prevention Planning Misidentified
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner detected system reconnaissance at line 385. Upon review, this section is titled 'Prevention Plan' and discusses processes to avoid accumulating new technical debt - not system reconnaissance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section discusses debt prevention strategies, not reconnaissance. This is a clear false positive.

content.value_statement: This skill helps development teams identify, quantify, and prioritize technical debt in their codebase. It provides structured methodologies for assessing debt impact, calculating ROI for remediation efforts, and creating actionable plans to improve code quality over time.
actual_capabilities: Conduct comprehensive technical debt inventories across code, architecture, testing, documentation, and infrastructure categories
Calculate ROI and cost impact for each debt item using developer hourly rates and time savings
Create prioritized remediation roadmaps with quick wins, medium-term improvements, and long-term initiatives
Design automated quality gates and debt budgets to prevent accumulation of new debt
Generate stakeholder-ready reports with metrics, risks, and executive summaries
limitations: Does not execute code changes - provides guidance only
Does not access external systems or perform network requests
Requires human judgment for final prioritization decisions
Metrics and calculations require manual verification for accuracy

use_cases.title: Initial Debt Assessment
use_cases.description: Run a comprehensive scan of an existing codebase to document all technical debt, categorize by type, and prioritize based on business impact.
use_cases.target_user: Tech leads and engineering managers seeking visibility into codebase health

use_cases.title: Sprint Planning Support
use_cases.description: Identify quick wins and high-ROI refactoring tasks to include in upcoming sprint planning sessions.
use_cases.target_user: Development teams allocating time for debt reduction in sprints

use_cases.title: Stakeholder Communication
use_cases.description: Generate executive summaries and ROI projections to justify technical debt remediation investments to business leadership.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering directors and VPs making budget decisions

prompt_templates.title: Quick Debt Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my codebase for technical debt. Focus on finding: duplicated code patterns, high complexity functions, missing tests, and outdated dependencies. List the top 10 debt items with estimated remediation effort.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user wanting a quick overview of debt hotspots

prompt_templates.title: Comprehensive Debt Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Conduct a full technical debt audit of our codebase. Include: code debt (duplication, complexity, structure), architecture debt (design flaws, tech stack), testing debt (coverage, quality), documentation debt, and infrastructure debt. For each category, quantify the impact and create a prioritized remediation plan.

prompt_templates.title: ROI-Focused Prioritization
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate the ROI for our top 20 technical debt items. For each, estimate: developer time lost per month, bug rate impact, and onboarding delay. Rank by highest ROI opportunity and create a quarterly remediation roadmap.

prompt_templates.title: Debt Prevention Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a debt prevention strategy for our team. Include: pre-commit hooks for code quality, CI pipeline gates, code review requirements, and a debt budget policy. Explain how to track compliance and measure success.
prompt_templates.scenario: Establishing processes to prevent future debt accumulation

output_examples.input: Analyze my Ruby on Rails codebase for technical debt
output_examples.output:
## Technical Debt Analysis

### Key Findings
- **Critical**: 3 God classes in app/models (500+ lines each)
- **High**: 23% code duplication in validation logic
- **Medium**: Test coverage at 45% (target: 80%)

### Top Quick Wins
1. Extract duplicate validation to shared module (8 hours, saves 20 hours/month)
2. Add error monitoring to payment service (4 hours, saves 15 hours/month)

### Recommended Roadmap
- **Month 1**: Quick wins + begin OrderService refactor
- **Month 2-3**: Complete refactoring + upgrade Rails version
- **Quarter 2**: Comprehensive test coverage improvement

output_examples.input: Create a debt prevention strategy for our team
output_examples.output:
## Debt Prevention Strategy

### Automated Gates
- Pre-commit: Complexity check (max 10), duplication check (max 5%)
- CI: Dependency audit, performance regression tests
- Code Review: Two approvals, tests required, documentation required

### Debt Budget
- Allowed monthly increase: 2%
- Mandatory quarterly reduction: 5%
- Tracking tools: SonarQube, Dependabot, CodeCov

### Success Metrics
- Monthly: Debt score reduction target -5%
- Quarterly: Architecture health score, developer satisfaction

best_practices: Start with quick wins that show immediate ROI to build team buy-in for larger refactoring efforts
Always quantify debt impact in developer hours and currency to communicate with stakeholders effectively
Use feature flags for gradual refactoring to reduce risk and enable incremental value delivery
anti_patterns: Attempting to fix all debt at once - prioritize based on impact and effort for sustainable progress
Ignoring testing debt - low coverage compounds all other debt types and slows future changes
Not tracking debt over time - without metrics, teams cannot measure improvement or regression

faq.question: What types of technical debt does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: The skill analyzes five main categories: code debt (duplication, complexity, structure), architecture debt (design flaws, technology stack), testing debt (coverage gaps, test quality), documentation debt (missing docs, outdated guides), and infrastructure debt (deployment issues, monitoring gaps).

faq.question: Does this skill make changes to my codebase?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides analysis, recommendations, and guidance. It does not execute any code changes. All refactoring work must be performed manually or with other tools.

faq.question: How does the skill calculate ROI for debt remediation?
faq.answer: ROI is calculated by estimating the time saved per month from fixing the debt, multiplied by the developer hourly rate, compared to the effort required to implement the fix. Default rate is $150/hour but can be adjusted.

faq.question: Can this skill work with any programming language?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill provides a framework for analyzing debt that is language-agnostic. The specific metrics and tools mentioned can be adapted to any technology stack.

faq.question: How often should we run technical debt analysis?
faq.answer: Teams should conduct comprehensive analysis quarterly, with monthly quick scans to track progress. After major releases or significant refactoring, additional analysis helps identify new debt accumulation.

faq.question: What is a debt budget and how do we implement one?
faq.answer: A debt budget limits how much new debt can be introduced per quarter (typically 2-5%). It requires teams to fix existing debt before adding new debt. Implementation uses automated tools to track complexity, duplication, and coverage metrics.
